full_response:
We work in the Earth rest‐frame throughout.  
(The problem explicitly tells us to ignore the short periods of acceleration, so while the ship is in flight it may be treated as an inertial frame moving at constant speed v = 0.9 c.)

1. Proper time elapsed on the ship before the flash  
   τship = 30 min = 0.5 h.

2. Time dilation (outbound speed v = 0.9 c)  
   γ = 1 / √(1 − β²) ,  β = v/c = 0.9  
   γ = 1 / √(1 − 0.9²) = 1 / √0.19 ≃ 2.29416.

   Earth‐frame time at the instant of emission  
   tE,emit = γ τship = 2.29416 × 0.5 h = 1.14708 h.

3. Position of the ship (and of the flash) at the moment of emission  
   xemit = v tE,emit = (0.9 c)(1.14708 h)  
          = 0.9 × 1.14708 light-hours = 1.03237 light-hours.

4. The flash is light, so it returns to Earth at speed c.  
   Travel time back to Earth  
   Δtlight = xemit / c = 1.03237 h.

5. Earth‐frame reception time, measured from launch  
   tE,recv = tE,emit + Δtlight  
           = 1.14708 h + 1.03237 h  
           = 2.17945 h.

Convert to ordinary units:

2.17945 h = 2 h + 0.17945 h  
           = 2 h + (0.17945 × 60 min)  
           = 2 h 10 min 45.5 s.

\boxed{t_{\text{Earth,\,launch→reception}}\;\approx\;2.18\ \text{hours}\;(\;2\ \text{h}\,10\ \text{min}\,46\ \text{s}\,)} 

Confidence: 90%
